name: Monaco
tagline: A rock above the sea held for seven centuries by one family, the longest-reigning dynasty in Europe.
summary: Monaco's history is the story of how a single fortified rock on the Ligurian coast became a sovereign principality and stayed one against far greater powers. The harbour was known to the Greeks as Monoikos, sacred to Hercules, and to the Romans as Portus Herculis Monoeci. The modern state began as a Genoese fortress founded in 1215, but its defining moment came on 8 January 1297, when François Grimaldi, called Malizia, seized the Rock disguised as a Franciscan monk, beginning a Grimaldi rule that has never ended. For centuries the lords and then princes of Monaco preserved a precarious independence between Genoa, France, Spain and Savoy, sheltering under Spanish protection and then, by the Treaty of Péronne in 1641, under French. In 1861 Monaco ceded Menton and Roquebrune to France, losing the great bulk of its territory, but won recognition of its sovereignty and, in the founding of Monte-Carlo and its casino, the means to rebuild its fortunes. Treaties of 1918 and 2002 bound Monaco's sovereignty closely to France while guaranteeing its survival, and the principality, modernised under Rainier III and his wife Princess Grace, joined the United Nations in 1993 and today uses the euro.

founding.label: François Grimaldi seizes the Rock of Monaco
founding.detail: Monaco's founding moment is dated to the night of 8 January 1297, when François Grimaldi, a Genoese Guelph nicknamed Malizia ('the Cunning'), took the fortress on the Rock of Monaco from its Ghibelline garrison by disguising himself in the habit of a Franciscan friar and concealing his weapons beneath the robe. The ruse is commemorated to this day in the princely coat of arms, whose supporters are two monks bearing swords. Though the Grimaldi were driven out and only secured permanent possession in the decades that followed, the seizure of 1297 is taken as the origin of the Grimaldi presence in Monaco. The dynasty it founded still reigns, making the House of Grimaldi the longest-ruling family in Europe. The fortress they captured, begun by Genoa in 1215, remains the core of the Prince's Palace seven centuries later.

quickFacts.label: Founding seizure
quickFacts.value: François Grimaldi took the Rock disguised as a monk, 8 January 1297

quickFacts.label: Genoese fortress
quickFacts.value: Begun by Genoa on the Rock in 1215; still the core of the Prince's Palace

quickFacts.label: First prince
quickFacts.value: Honoré II adopted the title Prince of Monaco in 1612; French protection by the Treaty of Péronne, 1641

quickFacts.label: Great cession
quickFacts.value: Menton and Roquebrune ceded to France in 1861 for four million francs, recognising Monaco's sovereignty

quickFacts.label: United Nations
quickFacts.value: Admitted as the 183rd UN member state on 28 May 1993

quickFacts.label: Currency
quickFacts.value: Uses the euro under a monetary agreement with France; the longest-reigning dynasty in Europe still rules

eras.title: Monoikos: The Harbour of Hercules
eras.standfirst: A sheltered Ligurian harbour sacred to Hercules served Greek and Roman shipping long before any state called Monaco existed.
body: Long before the Grimaldi, the deep natural harbour beneath the Rock of Monaco was known to the ancient world. The earliest surviving reference comes from the Greek geographer Hecataeus of Miletus, who around 500 BCE described 'Monoikos, a Ligurian town'. The first inhabitants of the coast were the Ligurians, and the site's name is native and ancient. By Greek and later Roman times the place was firmly associated with the cult of Hercules: the name Monoikos was linked by ancient writers to the hero, and the harbour was understood as a place sacred to him.

The port lay on the busy maritime route between Italy, Gaul and Hispania, and archaeology confirms its integration into Mediterranean trade. The harbour saw intense activity around 300 BCE, and finds of coins, amphorae, anchors and shipwrecks attest to centuries of commerce. After Rome conquered the region in the second century BCE the port became Portus Herculis Monoeci, a refuge and waystation on the shipping lanes; Roman expeditions to Hispania passed this way from 137 BCE, and Julius Caesar is recorded to have used the harbour around 50 BCE on his way to confront Pompey.

With the decline of Roman power the coast fell into the long insecurity of the early Middle Ages, exposed to raiding and shifting overlordship. The Rock that commanded the harbour was a natural stronghold, and it was its strategic value, controlling a safe anchorage on a dangerous coast, that would draw the maritime republic of Genoa to fortify it in the thirteenth century, setting the stage for the principality to come.

events.title: Monoikos named by Hecataeus
events.summary: The Greek geographer Hecataeus of Miletus refers to Monoikos as a Ligurian town, the earliest known mention of the site that would become Monaco; later the Phocaeans of Massalia developed it as a port.

events.title: The harbour at its height
events.summary: The port of Monoikos, associated with the cult of Hercules, flourished as part of Mediterranean trade, as shown by coins and goods recovered from the harbour.

events.title: Portus Herculis Monoeci under Rome
events.summary: After Rome's conquest of the region the harbour became Portus Herculis Monoeci, a refuge on the route between Italy, Gaul and Hispania used by Roman expeditions.

events.title: Caesar uses the port
events.summary: Julius Caesar is recorded as having used the harbour of Monoeci around 50 BCE, a measure of its importance as a Roman anchorage on the Ligurian coast.

figures.name: Hecataeus of Miletus
figures.blurb: An early Greek geographer whose description of 'Monoikos, a Ligurian town' is the oldest surviving written reference to the site of Monaco, fixing its existence in the historical record before 500 BCE.

figures.name: Hercules (Monoikos)
figures.blurb: The harbour beneath the Rock was sacred to Hercules in both Greek and Roman belief; the Roman name Portus Herculis Monoeci preserved the cult, and the hero remained the place's mythic patron throughout antiquity.

pullquote.text: The earliest known reference to the site is to 'Monoikos, a Ligurian town', recorded by the Greek geographer Hecataeus around 500 BCE.
pullquote.attribution: On the antiquity of the harbour

eras.title: The Genoese Fortress and the Coming of the Grimaldi
eras.standfirst: Genoa fortified the Rock in 1215; in 1297 François Grimaldi took it by a famous ruse, founding a dynasty that has reigned ever since.
body: The modern history of Monaco begins with stone. In 1215 a detachment of Genoese Ghibellines led by Fulco del Cassello began to build a fortress on the Rock, raising four great towers joined by a curtain wall to command the harbour. To people the new stronghold they offered land and tax exemptions to settlers, founding the town beneath the walls. The fortress was a Genoese military outpost, and its possession was bound up in the long struggle between Guelphs, who supported the papacy, and Ghibellines, who supported the emperor, that convulsed the Italian republics.

The Grimaldi were a leading Guelph family of Genoa, driven into exile when the Ghibellines seized control of the city. On the night of 8 January 1297 François Grimaldi, called Malizia, took the fortress of Monaco by stealth, entering disguised as a Franciscan monk with his sword hidden beneath the friar's habit. The audacity of the deed entered legend and heraldry alike: the supporters of the princely arms are two armed monks. The Grimaldi did not hold the Rock without interruption; possession was contested for decades, and in 1301 the family had to come to terms with Charles II of Anjou, count of Provence.

It was Charles I Grimaldi, in the first half of the fourteenth century, who is regarded as the true founder of the lordship. He secured the family's authority over the Rock, took the title of lord, and enlarged the domain by purchasing the neighbouring lordships of Menton in 1346 and Roquebrune in 1355, which would remain Monégasque until 1861. From this small but strategic territory the Grimaldi began the long effort, lasting centuries, to keep their independence intact between the greater powers of Genoa, France, Provence, Savoy and, later, Spain.

events.title: Genoa fortifies the Rock
events.summary: Genoese Ghibellines under Fulco del Cassello began building a fortress of four towers on the Rock of Monaco, offering land and tax exemptions to draw settlers to the new stronghold.

events.title: Grimaldi seizes the Rock
events.summary: François Grimaldi, called Malizia, took the fortress disguised as a Franciscan monk, beginning the Grimaldi presence in Monaco, an event commemorated by the armed monks of the princely coat of arms.

events.title: Purchase of Menton
events.summary: Charles I Grimaldi, regarded as the founder of the principality, enlarged the Grimaldi domain by purchasing the lordship of Menton, which remained Monégasque until 1861.

events.title: Purchase of Roquebrune
events.summary: The Grimaldi added the lordship of Roquebrune to their possessions, completing the territory that, with Monaco and Menton, would define the principality for five centuries.

figures.name: François Grimaldi ('Malizia')
figures.role: Founder of the Grimaldi presence in Monaco
figures.blurb: A Genoese Guelph leader whose seizure of the Rock of Monaco on 8 January 1297, disguised as a Franciscan monk, founded the Grimaldi dynasty. His cunning earned him the nickname Malizia and gave the princely arms their armed monks.

figures.name: Charles I Grimaldi
figures.blurb: Considered the real founder of the principality, Charles I secured Grimaldi authority over the Rock, adopted the title of lord, and bought Menton and Roquebrune, giving Monaco the territory it would hold until 1861.

pullquote.text: On the night of 8 January 1297 François Grimaldi took the fortress of Monaco by stealth, entering disguised as a Franciscan monk.
pullquote.attribution: Government of Monaco, on the founding seizure

eras.title: From Lordship to Principality: Spanish, then French Protection
eras.standfirst: The Grimaldi turned a contested lordship into a sovereign principality, sheltering first under Spain and then, by the Treaty of Péronne in 1641, under France.
body: Through the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries the Grimaldi consolidated their grip on the Rock and worked to have their independence acknowledged by their neighbours. Survival meant playing the great powers against one another. In the early sixteenth century, with France and Spain contending for mastery of Italy, the lords of Monaco placed themselves under the protection of Spain, whose Mediterranean power could shield the tiny state. The fortress was steadily transformed from a military stronghold into a princely residence; the architects employed by Honoré I and Honoré II turned it into one of the most remarkable seventeenth-century palaces in the region, the building that still stands above the harbour.

In 1612 Honoré II became the first of his line to assume the title Prince of Monaco, marking the transformation of the medieval lordship into a sovereign principality. But Spanish protection had become burdensome, and the wider conflict of the Thirty Years' War offered an opening. Cardinal Richelieu's France had been cultivating Monaco since around 1630, prizing the Rock as a bridgehead into Italy. In a carefully prepared move, Honoré II expelled the Spanish garrison and turned to France.

The realignment was sealed by the Treaty of Péronne, signed on 14 September 1641 between Honoré II and Louis XIII. The treaty removed Monaco from Spanish protection and placed it under that of France, while formally recognising the independence and sovereignty of the principality and the prince's rights over Monaco, Menton and Roquebrune. A French garrison, paid by the royal treasury but under the prince's orders, would guarantee the principality's security. Honoré II also received French titles and estates, including the duchy of Valentinois. For a century and a half the Péronne settlement defined Monaco as a small sovereign state living under French patronage.

events.title: Honoré II takes the title of Prince
events.summary: Honoré II became the first Grimaldi to call himself Prince and Lord of Monaco, marking the principality's emergence as a sovereign state out of the medieval lordship.

events.title: Treaty of Péronne
events.summary: Honoré II and Louis XIII signed the Treaty of Péronne, shifting Monaco from Spanish to French protection while recognising the principality's independence and the prince's sovereignty.

events.title: French titles for the Grimaldi
events.summary: Under the Péronne settlement the prince of Monaco received French titles and estates, including the duchy of Valentinois, binding the dynasty to the French crown.

events.title: The fortress becomes a palace
events.summary: Honoré I and Honoré II employed leading architects to transform the Genoese fortress into a princely residence, creating one of the most remarkable palaces of the seventeenth century.

figures.name: Honoré II
figures.blurb: Honoré II adopted the title of Prince in 1612 and in 1641 negotiated the Treaty of Péronne, expelling the Spanish garrison and placing Monaco under French protection while securing recognition of its sovereignty.

figures.name: Louis XIII of France
figures.role: King of France, guarantor of Péronne
figures.blurb: King of France whose government, guided by Cardinal Richelieu, drew Monaco out of the Spanish orbit. By the Treaty of Péronne of 1641 he recognised Monaco's sovereignty and extended French protection over the principality.

pullquote.text: The Treaty of Péronne formally recognised the independence of the Principality, placing it under French protection with its sovereignty guaranteed.
pullquote.attribution: Government of Monaco, on the 1641 treaty

eras.title: Revolution, the Cession of 1861 and the Birth of Monte-Carlo
eras.standfirst: Annexed by revolutionary France, then restored, Monaco lost Menton and Roquebrune in 1861 but won recognition of its sovereignty and rebuilt its fortunes on the casino of Monte-Carlo.
body: The French Revolution swept the principality away. In 1793 revolutionary France annexed Monaco, abolished the Grimaldi sovereignty and dispersed the palace's treasures; the Rock was turned to other uses and the dynasty driven out. The Congress of Vienna in 1815 restored the Grimaldi but placed Monaco under the protection of the Kingdom of Sardinia rather than France, an arrangement that satisfied no one. Discontent festered in the two larger towns of the principality, Menton and Roquebrune, whose people resented Grimaldi taxation.

In the revolutionary year of 1848 Menton and Roquebrune declared themselves free towns, banished the prince and his descendants, and sought union with Sardinia, which occupied them. The crisis was resolved only when France acquired Nice from Sardinia. By the Franco-Monégasque Treaty of 2 February 1861, Prince Charles III renounced all rights to Menton and Roquebrune, which passed to France, in return for a payment of four million francs and the promise of road and rail links. The cession cost Monaco the overwhelming majority of its territory, but France's recognition of Monégasque sovereignty was its enduring gain, and a customs union followed in 1865.

Shorn of its farmland and revenues, Monaco gambled on a new economy. Charles III had already, by royal decree of 1863, established the Société des Bains de Mer to develop a new quarter and a casino, leasing the gambling monopoly to the entrepreneur François Blanc, who had made the German spa of Homburg prosper. The venture flourished: by 1869 some 170,000 visitors a year were coming, and on 1 June 1866 the prince decreed that the new district be named Monte-Carlo in his own honour. So profitable was the casino that in 1869 the Grimaldi abolished direct taxation on residents, the origin of Monaco's enduring identity as a tax-free haven. The gambling revenues rebuilt the principality's finances and underwrote its survival as a state.

events.title: Revolutionary France annexes Monaco
events.summary: Revolutionary France abolished the Grimaldi sovereignty and annexed the principality, expelling the dynasty and scattering the palace's art collections.

events.title: Menton and Roquebrune secede
events.summary: Amid the revolutions of 1848, Menton and Roquebrune declared themselves free towns, banished the prince and sought union with Sardinia, which then occupied them.

events.title: Cession of Menton and Roquebrune
events.summary: By the Franco-Monégasque Treaty, Prince Charles III ceded Menton and Roquebrune to France for four million francs; France recognised Monaco's sovereignty over its remaining territory.

events.title: Founding of the Société des Bains de Mer
events.summary: Charles III established the Société des Bains de Mer to develop a casino quarter, leasing the gambling monopoly to François Blanc, who had previously enriched the spa of Homburg.

events.title: Monte-Carlo named
events.summary: Prince Charles III decreed that the new casino district be called Monte-Carlo in his honour; its gambling revenues rebuilt Monaco's finances and let the principality abolish direct taxation in 1869.

figures.name: Charles III
figures.blurb: The prince who ceded Menton and Roquebrune to France in 1861 in exchange for recognition of Monaco's sovereignty and four million francs, then refounded the principality's fortunes by creating the casino and the new district of Monte-Carlo, named for him in 1866.

figures.name: François Blanc
figures.blurb: Master of the German spa casino at Homburg, Blanc took the lease of Monaco's gambling monopoly through the Société des Bains de Mer from 1863, turning Monte-Carlo into the most famous casino resort in Europe and the financial engine of the principality.

pullquote.text: Prince Charles III waived all rights to Menton and Roquebrune in exchange for compensation of four million francs and the opening of the Principality by road and rail.
pullquote.attribution: Government of Monaco, on the 1861 treaty

eras.title: Sovereignty Tied to France and the Making of the Modern Principality
eras.standfirst: Treaties of 1918 and 2002 bound Monaco's independence closely to France while guaranteeing its survival; under Rainier III and Princess Grace the principality modernised, joined the UN and adopted the euro.
body: The twentieth century saw Monaco become a constitutional state and tie its sovereignty ever more explicitly to France. Prince Albert I granted Monaco's first constitution in 1911, ending absolute princely rule. During the First World War a succession crisis loomed: the heir had no legitimate children, and the prospect that the throne might pass to a German cousin, a Duke of Urach, alarmed France. The result was the Franco-Monégasque Treaty signed on 17 July 1918, which placed Monaco's foreign policy in harmony with French interests and provided that, should the Grimaldi line die out, the principality would come under French protection. This treaty was noted in the Treaty of Versailles of 1919, giving Monaco's special status international standing.

After the Second World War Monaco's modern era opened with the reign of Rainier III, who succeeded in 1949. When he came to the throne gambling still provided the great bulk of state revenue. Rainier diversified the economy, reclaimed land from the sea to build the Fontvieille district, and in 1962 promulgated a new constitution that reduced the sovereign's powers, abolished capital punishment and granted women the vote. His marriage in 1956 to the American film star Grace Kelly, who became Princess Grace, brought Monaco worldwide glamour and helped transform it into a byword for wealth and luxury until her death in a car accident in 1982.

In its final decades the principality secured both its place in the world and the permanence of its independence. Monaco was admitted to the United Nations as its 183rd member state on 28 May 1993, a definitive assertion of sovereign statehood, and it adopted the euro under a monetary agreement with France. A new Franco-Monégasque treaty signed on 24 October 2002, accompanying a revision of the constitution and the succession law, removed the old provision by which Monaco would revert to France if the Grimaldi line failed: henceforth the principality would remain independent regardless of the dynasty's continuity. Prince Albert II succeeded his father Rainier III in 2005, the latest sovereign of a house that has reigned, with interruptions only by revolution, since 1297.

events.title: Monaco's first constitution
events.summary: Prince Albert I granted Monaco a constitution on 5 January 1911, ending absolute princely rule and beginning the principality's evolution into a constitutional state.

events.title: Franco-Monégasque Treaty of 1918
events.summary: Signed amid a succession crisis, the treaty aligned Monaco's policy with French interests and provided that the principality would fall under French protection should the Grimaldi line end; it was noted in the 1919 Treaty of Versailles.

events.title: Rainier III marries Grace Kelly
events.summary: Prince Rainier III married the American actress Grace Kelly, who became Princess Grace; their union brought the principality international glamour and helped drive its post-war revival.

events.title: The 1962 constitution
events.summary: Rainier III promulgated a new constitution that reduced the sovereign's powers, abolished capital punishment and granted women the right to vote.

events.title: Monaco joins the United Nations
events.summary: Monaco was admitted as the 183rd member state of the United Nations, an unambiguous international recognition of its full sovereignty.

events.title: Treaty of 2002 guarantees independence
events.summary: A new Franco-Monégasque treaty, with the revised constitution and succession law, ended the clause returning Monaco to France if the Grimaldi line failed, guaranteeing the principality's continued independence.

figures.name: Rainier III
figures.blurb: The 31st hereditary ruler, who reigned from 1949 to 2005, Rainier III modernised Monaco's economy beyond gambling, reclaimed land for the Fontvieille district and gave the principality the constitution of 1962. His marriage to Grace Kelly made Monaco internationally famous.

figures.name: Princess Grace (Grace Kelly)
figures.blurb: The American Academy Award-winning actress who married Rainier III in 1956 and became Princess Grace of Monaco. Her glamour transformed the principality's image; she died in a car accident in 1982.

pullquote.text: Monaco became the 183rd member state of the United Nations in May 1993, and a treaty of 2002 ensured its independence would survive even if the Grimaldi line ended.

figures.name: François Grimaldi ('Malizia')
figures.blurb: The Genoese Guelph who seized the Rock of Monaco on 8 January 1297 disguised as a Franciscan monk, beginning a Grimaldi rule that endures to this day as the longest of any reigning house in Europe.

figures.name: Honoré II
figures.blurb: Took the title of Prince in 1612 and by the Treaty of Péronne in 1641 shifted Monaco from Spanish to French protection, securing recognition of the principality's sovereignty.

figures.name: Charles III
figures.blurb: Ceded Menton and Roquebrune to France in 1861 in exchange for recognition of Monaco's sovereignty, then rebuilt its finances by founding the casino and the district of Monte-Carlo, named for him in 1866.

figures.name: Rainier III
figures.blurb: Reigning from 1949 to 2005, he gave Monaco the 1962 constitution, diversified its economy, oversaw its admission to the United Nations in 1993, and through his marriage to Grace Kelly made the principality world-famous.
